description: A diagram showing the agent-environment interaction loop: an agent observes state and reward from the environment, then takes an action, which feeds back into the environment.
teaching_value: Gives students an immediate visual anchor for the core RL feedback loop before any formalism is introduced, making abstract concepts like state, action, and reward concrete.
when_to_show: Show at the very start of any RL introduction, before defining formal terms, to ground the intuition of the learning loop.
suggested_caption: Agent-environment interaction loop: state, action, reward cycle. (Lilian Weng)

description: A taxonomy diagram categorizing RL algorithms into model-based vs model-free, and within model-free into value-based, policy-based, and actor-critic approaches, with example algorithms placed in each category.
teaching_value: Helps students navigate the RL landscape by showing how major algorithm families relate to each other, preventing confusion when encountering new algorithm names.
when_to_show: Show after introducing basic RL concepts and before diving into specific algorithms, as a roadmap students can return to throughout the course.
suggested_caption: RL algorithm taxonomy: model-based vs. model-free, value vs. policy. (David Silver)

description: The classic Sutton & Barto diagram of the MDP agent-environment interaction cycle, showing the agent receiving state S_t and reward R_t at each timestep and outputting action A_t.
teaching_value: Formalizes the RL loop into MDP notation with explicit timestep indexing, bridging intuitive understanding to mathematical formulation of states, actions, and rewards.
when_to_show: Show when introducing MDPs formally, after students understand the intuitive loop, to connect intuition to the mathematical framework.
suggested_caption: MDP agent-environment interaction with timestep notation. (Sutton & Barto 2017)

description: A fun illustrative MDP example using a typical work day: states include 'home', 'office', 'lunch', etc., with actions and transitions shown as arrows, and rewards annotated on transitions.
teaching_value: Makes the abstract MDP formalism tangible by mapping it onto a relatable real-world scenario, helping students identify states, actions, transitions, and rewards in everyday contexts.
when_to_show: Show immediately after introducing the formal MDP definition to provide an accessible worked example before moving to mathematical derivations.
suggested_caption: Work-day MDP example: states, actions, and rewards illustrated. (randomant.net)

description: Backup diagrams illustrating Bellman expectation equations for both state-value V(s) and action-value Q(s,a) functions, showing how current values are computed from successor states and rewards.
teaching_value: Visually demonstrates the recursive decomposition at the heart of Bellman equations, making it clear how value propagates backward through the state-action tree.
when_to_show: Show when deriving Bellman equations, using the diagram to trace through the one-step lookahead and explain the expectation over next states.
suggested_caption: Bellman backup diagrams for V(s) and Q(s,a) value functions. (Lilian Weng)

description: Backup diagrams comparing Q-learning (off-policy) and SARSA (on-policy) TD control, showing the difference in which next action is used for the update target.
teaching_value: Visually highlights the key algorithmic difference between on-policy and off-policy TD control: SARSA uses the action actually taken, while Q-learning uses the greedy max action.
when_to_show: Show when introducing TD control methods to visually contrast how SARSA and Q-learning differ in their update targets before presenting the pseudocode.
suggested_caption: Backup diagrams contrasting Q-learning and SARSA update targets. (Sutton & Barto 2017)

description: Side-by-side backup diagrams for Q-learning and SARSA, explicitly labeling the greedy max selection in Q-learning versus the policy-sampled next action in SARSA.
teaching_value: Provides a direct visual comparison that makes the on-policy vs off-policy distinction concrete, reinforcing why Q-learning converges to optimal policy regardless of behavior policy.
when_to_show: Show side-by-side when students struggle to articulate the difference between SARSA and Q-learning, pointing to the branching structure to explain the update rule.
suggested_caption: Q-learning (off-policy) vs. SARSA (on-policy) backup diagrams compared. (Sutton & Barto 2017)

description: The full DQN algorithm pseudocode from Mnih et al. 2015, including experience replay buffer initialization, epsilon-greedy action selection, minibatch sampling, and periodic frozen target network updates.
teaching_value: Provides the complete, authoritative algorithm students need to implement DQN, highlighting the two key stabilization techniques: experience replay and frozen target networks.
when_to_show: Show when teaching DQN after explaining why naive Q-learning with neural networks is unstable, using each step of the pseudocode to explain how replay and frozen targets address instability.
suggested_caption: DQN algorithm with experience replay and frozen target network. (Mnih et al. 2015)

description: A diagram comparing backup depth across TD(λ), showing how λ-return interpolates between one-step TD (shallow backup) and full Monte-Carlo (deep backup) using exponentially decaying weights.
teaching_value: Visually explains how TD(λ) unifies TD and MC methods on a spectrum, helping students understand why λ is a powerful hyperparameter controlling bias-variance tradeoff.
when_to_show: Show when introducing TD(λ) after students understand both 1-step TD and MC, to illustrate how λ interpolates between the two extremes.
suggested_caption: TD(λ) backup depth interpolates between TD and Monte-Carlo methods. (David Silver)

description: Three backup diagrams side by side comparing Monte-Carlo (full episode depth), TD learning (one-step depth), and Dynamic Programming (full-width one-step), illustrating the depth and width of each method's update.
teaching_value: Crystallizes the fundamental differences among the three core RL value estimation approaches along two axes—depth (how far ahead) and width (sample vs. expectation)—in a single visual.
when_to_show: Show as a synthesis diagram after covering all three methods, or use it as a preview at the start to give students a conceptual map of where each method sits.
suggested_caption: MC, TD, and DP backup diagrams: depth and width compared. (David Silver)

description: A flowchart showing a parallel Evolution Strategies RL algorithm where a master node distributes random seeds to multiple parallel workers, each worker evaluates a perturbed policy in the environment, and returns scalar rewards back to the master for parameter updates. Workers share random seeds to reconstruct Gaussian noise without transmitting full gradient vectors.
teaching_value: Illustrates how ES avoids gradient backpropagation by using shared random seeds for noise reconstruction, making the communication overhead minimal. Students can see concretely how parallelism is achieved and why ES is bandwidth-efficient compared to gradient-based distributed RL.
when_to_show: Show when introducing Evolution Strategies as an alternative to MDP/TD-based RL, specifically when explaining how ES scales efficiently via parallelism and why shared random seeds reduce communication cost.
suggested_caption: Parallel ES-RL: workers share seeds to reconstruct noise cheaply. (Salimans et al., 2017)

description: A diagram of a 19x19 Go board showing the placement of black and white stones, illustrating key game concepts: liberties (open intersection points adjacent to a stone group), eyes (enclosed liberties needed for a group to stay alive), and the positional superko rule.
teaching_value: Grounds the abstract AlphaGo Zero discussion in the concrete rules and structure of Go, helping students understand why Go is computationally hard (large state space, complex survival rules) and what the RL agent must learn to master.
when_to_show: Show at the start of the AlphaGo Zero case study, before discussing the neural network architecture or MCTS, to ensure students understand the environment the RL agent operates in.
suggested_caption: 19×19 Go board: liberties and eyes define stone survival rules.

description: A diagram showing the AlphaGo Zero self-play training loop: the current neural network policy plays games against itself via MCTS, generating training data (board states, MCTS policy targets, game outcomes), which are used to update the network weights, and the improved network is then used for the next round of self-play.
teaching_value: Visually clarifies the bootstrapping self-improvement cycle central to AlphaGo Zero — students can see how MCTS acts as a policy improvement operator at inference time while the neural network is trained to imitate MCTS outputs, illustrating the interplay between learned value/policy functions and tree search.
when_to_show: Show when explaining how AlphaGo Zero trains purely from self-play, especially to contrast with supervised learning from human expert data used in the original AlphaGo, and to illustrate how MCTS and neural networks interact iteratively.
suggested_caption: AlphaGo Zero self-play loop: MCTS refines policy each step. (Silver et al., 2017)

description: Mathematical expression showing the general form of the policy gradient, with expectation over state-action distributions under policy π, combining log probability of action and a function Ψ representing various return estimators
teaching_value: Shows students the unified mathematical framework from which all policy gradient algorithms derive, making it clear that different algorithms (REINFORCE, A2C, etc.) are special cases differing only in their choice of Ψ
when_to_show: Introduce this immediately after deriving the policy gradient theorem, before discussing specific algorithms, to anchor all subsequent methods to a common formula
suggested_caption: General policy gradient form; Ψ varies by algorithm. (Schulman et al., 2016)

description: Side-by-side architectural diagram comparing A3C (asynchronous) and A2C (synchronous) showing multiple parallel actors, their interaction with a global network, and the role of a coordinator in A2C that synchronizes gradient updates before applying them
teaching_value: Visually clarifies the key structural difference between A3C and A2C: asynchronous independent updates vs. synchronized batch updates, helping students understand why A2C can be more stable and GPU-efficient
when_to_show: Show when transitioning from A3C to A2C to highlight the single architectural change (coordinator/synchronization) that distinguishes the two methods
suggested_caption: A3C uses async updates; A2C synchronizes all actors before updating.

description: Pseudocode for the DDPG algorithm detailing initialization of critic and actor networks with random weights, replay buffer setup, soft target network updates, exploration noise addition, and the actor-critic update steps per episode
teaching_value: Provides a concrete, step-by-step implementation blueprint for DDPG, showing how soft target updates (τ≪1), experience replay, and exploration noise work together in a continuous action space setting
when_to_show: Present after explaining the motivation for DDPG (continuous actions, off-policy learning), so students can map each algorithmic step to the conceptual components already discussed
suggested_caption: DDPG algorithm with soft target updates and exploration noise. (Lillicrap et al., 2015)

description: Pseudocode for the D4PG algorithm showing distributed actors feeding a shared replay buffer, distributional critic updates using N-step returns, and priority-weighted sampling, extending DDPG with distributional and distributed components
teaching_value: Illustrates how D4PG extends DDPG with three concrete improvements—distributional critic, N-step returns, and K parallel actors—making the algorithmic differences explicit and traceable in code form
when_to_show: Show after DDPG algorithm is understood; use to walk through each of the three D4PG improvements one by one, pointing to the corresponding pseudocode lines
suggested_caption: D4PG extends DDPG with distributional critic and N parallel actors. (Barth-Maron et al., 2018)

description: Architecture diagram of MADDPG showing multiple agents each with their own actor (using local observations) and a centralized critic (using global observations and all agents' actions), illustrating the centralized training with decentralized execution paradigm
teaching_value: Visually demonstrates the core MADDPG design principle: critics have global information during training while actors only use local observations during execution, helping students understand how non-stationarity is addressed in multi-agent settings
when_to_show: Show when introducing multi-agent RL challenges; use the diagram to explain why a centralized critic stabilizes training even when agents act independently at test time
suggested_caption: MADDPG: centralized critics, decentralized actors per agent. (Lowe et al., 2017)

description: Pseudocode for the PPG (Phasic Policy Gradient) algorithm showing two alternating training phases: a policy phase updating the policy network with PPO, and an auxiliary phase updating the value network with distillation loss to prevent policy drift
teaching_value: Makes concrete how PPG separates policy and value function training into distinct phases, allowing students to see exactly how the auxiliary phase uses behavioral cloning loss (β_clone) to prevent the policy from diverging while the value network trains
when_to_show: Present after explaining the parameter-sharing trade-off in actor-critic methods; use the two-phase structure to show how PPG resolves the conflict between policy and value objectives
suggested_caption: PPG alternates policy and auxiliary phases to decouple training. (Cobbe et al., 2020)

description: Bar chart or learning curve comparing mean normalized performance of PPG versus PPO across multiple Procgen benchmark environments, showing PPG achieving higher sample efficiency
teaching_value: Provides empirical evidence that the architectural separation in PPG translates to measurable performance gains, helping students connect the theoretical motivation to real benchmark results
when_to_show: Show immediately after the PPG algorithm diagram to validate the claim that phase separation improves sample efficiency; prompt students to identify which environments benefit most
suggested_caption: PPG outperforms PPO in sample efficiency on Procgen. (Cobbe et al., 2020)

description: Pseudocode for the Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) algorithm showing entropy-regularized policy optimization with soft Q-function updates, policy update via reparameterization, and fixed temperature parameter α balancing reward and entropy
teaching_value: Shows students how maximum entropy RL is implemented in practice, making explicit the three interleaved updates (Q-functions, policy, temperature) and how the entropy bonus is incorporated into the objective
when_to_show: Introduce after explaining the maximum entropy RL objective; walk through each update step to show how the entropy term modifies standard actor-critic updates
suggested_caption: SAC algorithm with entropy regularization and fixed temperature α. (Haarnoja et al., 2018)

description: Pseudocode for SAC with automatically adjusted temperature, adding a gradient-based update rule for α that minimizes the difference between current entropy and a target entropy threshold, removing the need for manual temperature tuning
teaching_value: Demonstrates how automatic temperature tuning is implemented as a simple additional gradient step, helping students understand that the temperature becomes a learned parameter with its own loss function derived from the dual optimization problem
when_to_show: Show after the fixed-temperature SAC algorithm; highlight the single added line updating α and explain why this resolves the brittleness of manual temperature selection across different tasks
suggested_caption: SAC with auto-tuned temperature α via dual gradient descent. (Haarnoja et al., 2018)

description: Pseudocode/algorithm box for TD3 (Twin Delayed Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient), detailing initialization, actor-critic network setup, replay buffer, and the training loop including target policy smoothing with clipped noise, twin critic updates, and delayed actor/target network updates.
teaching_value: Gives students a concrete, step-by-step implementation blueprint for TD3, making abstract concepts like target policy smoothing, twin critics, and delayed updates tangible and sequentially ordered.
when_to_show: Show after explaining each of TD3's three core improvements (clipped double Q-learning, delayed updates, target policy smoothing) so students can see how all three are integrated into a single coherent algorithm.
suggested_caption: TD3 algorithm pseudocode showing twin critics, delayed updates, and policy smoothing. (Fujimoto et al., 2018)

description: Architecture diagram of IMPALA showing multiple actor machines generating experience trajectories in parallel and sending them to a central learner, which updates policy and value function parameters and periodically broadcasts updated weights back to actors.
teaching_value: Visually clarifies the decoupled acting-learning architecture of IMPALA, helping students understand how parallelism is achieved and why an off-policy correction (V-trace) is necessary due to the policy lag between actors and learner.
when_to_show: Show when introducing IMPALA's motivation for scalability, before diving into V-trace math, so students first grasp the high-level system design and understand why the behavior and training policies diverge.
suggested_caption: IMPALA architecture: parallel actors feed a central learner with off-policy correction.

description: Line chart showing average episodic returns over training steps for CleanRL's ppo.py compared against OpenAI Baselines' PPO across multiple classic control or MuJoCo environments, plotted against environment steps.
teaching_value: Allows students to verify that a clean, readable PPO implementation matches a well-established reference implementation, reinforcing that algorithmic correctness can be validated through benchmark comparison.
when_to_show: Show when teaching students how to validate a new RL implementation — use it to discuss the importance of reproducing baseline results before claiming novelty.
suggested_caption: CleanRL ppo.py vs. OpenAI Baselines PPO: average episodic returns over training steps.

description: Line chart showing average episodic returns for CleanRL's ppo.py plotted against wall-clock time (rather than environment steps), compared against OpenAI Baselines' PPO.
teaching_value: Demonstrates the difference between sample efficiency (steps-based) and computational efficiency (time-based) evaluation, helping students understand that two implementations can be sample-equivalent but differ in real-world training speed.
when_to_show: Show when discussing the distinction between sample efficiency and time efficiency, or when students ask why wall-clock time matters in addition to environment steps.
suggested_caption: CleanRL ppo.py vs. OpenAI Baselines PPO: episodic returns vs. wall-clock time.

description: Line chart showing average episodic returns over training steps for CleanRL's ppo_atari.py compared against OpenAI Baselines' PPO on Atari game environments.
teaching_value: Shows that PPO can be successfully applied to high-dimensional pixel-based observations (Atari), and that the CleanRL implementation reproduces baseline performance, illustrating the scalability of the algorithm.
when_to_show: Show when introducing PPO on image-based environments or when contrasting low-dimensional state spaces (classic control) with high-dimensional pixel inputs (Atari).
suggested_caption: CleanRL ppo_atari.py vs. OpenAI Baselines PPO on Atari: average episodic returns.

description: Line chart showing average episodic returns for CleanRL's ppo_atari.py plotted against wall-clock time, compared against OpenAI Baselines' PPO on Atari environments.
teaching_value: Highlights the computational cost of training PPO on Atari (pixel inputs require CNN processing), helping students appreciate the trade-off between environment complexity and training time.
when_to_show: Show when discussing the computational overhead of convolutional networks in RL, or when comparing training time across different environment types.
suggested_caption: CleanRL ppo_atari.py vs. OpenAI Baselines on Atari: episodic returns vs. wall-clock time.

description: Line chart showing average episodic returns over training steps for CleanRL's ppo_continuous_action.py across DeepMind Control Suite (dm_control) continuous-action environments.
teaching_value: Demonstrates PPO's applicability to continuous action spaces, helping students understand how the algorithm extends beyond discrete actions using a Gaussian policy parameterization.
when_to_show: Show when introducing PPO for continuous control tasks, or when contrasting discrete-action (Atari) and continuous-action (dm_control/MuJoCo) RL settings.
suggested_caption: CleanRL ppo_continuous_action.py on dm_control: average episodic returns over training steps.

description: Line chart showing average episodic returns for CleanRL's ppo_continuous_action.py plotted against wall-clock time across dm_control continuous-action environments.
teaching_value: Provides a time-based perspective on continuous-control PPO training, allowing students to compare computational efficiency across environment types and implementations.
when_to_show: Show alongside the steps-based chart (Image 5) to teach students how to interpret both sample efficiency and time efficiency for continuous control.
suggested_caption: CleanRL ppo_continuous_action.py on dm_control: episodic returns vs. wall-clock time.

description: Performance chart for ppo_continuous_action.py on Gymnasium-wrapped dm_control environments, including special note about lqr environments that are never terminated or truncated, comparing results across multiple tasks.
teaching_value: Illustrates an important edge case in RL environment design — environments without terminal states — and how this affects episodic return measurement, teaching students to be aware of environment-specific quirks.
when_to_show: Show when discussing environment wrappers, episode boundaries, or when students encounter environments that never terminate — use to highlight the importance of reading environment documentation.
suggested_caption: PPO on Gymnasium dm_control environments; note LQR tasks never terminate or truncate.

description: Comparison chart showing average episodic returns for ppo_continuous_action.py using MuJoCo v2 environments versus v4 environments in Gymnasium, demonstrating performance equivalence across versions.
teaching_value: Teaches students that environment versioning can affect results and that it is important to verify performance parity when migrating between API versions, a practical concern in reproducible RL research.
when_to_show: Show when discussing reproducibility in RL or when students ask about differences between gym and gymnasium, or between MuJoCo v2 and v4 environments.
suggested_caption: MuJoCo v2 vs. v4 environments: ppo_continuous_action.py performance is roughly equivalent.

description: Line chart showing average episodic returns over training steps for CleanRL's ppo_atari_lstm.py (PPO with LSTM recurrent policy) compared against OpenAI Baselines' PPO on Atari environments.
teaching_value: Demonstrates how adding an LSTM to PPO affects performance on Atari, helping students understand the role of memory/recurrence in handling partially observable environments.
when_to_show: Show when introducing recurrent policies in RL, or when discussing how memory can help agents in partially observable or temporally dependent tasks.
suggested_caption: CleanRL ppo_atari_lstm.py vs. OpenAI Baselines PPO on Atari: episodic returns with LSTM.

description: Line chart showing average episodic returns for CleanRL's ppo_atari_lstm.py plotted against wall-clock time, compared against OpenAI Baselines' PPO on Atari environments.
teaching_value: Reveals the additional computational cost of LSTM-based PPO compared to feedforward PPO, helping students understand the time-efficiency trade-off of using recurrent architectures.
when_to_show: Show alongside Image 9 to contrast sample efficiency with time efficiency for recurrent PPO, or when discussing the overhead of backpropagation through time (BPTT).
suggested_caption: CleanRL ppo_atari_lstm.py vs. OpenAI Baselines on Atari: episodic returns vs. wall-clock time.

description: Learning curves (episodic return vs. environment steps) for PPO with Atari EnvPool across multiple Atari games, showing performance benchmarks
teaching_value: Allows students to compare PPO's sample efficiency across diverse Atari environments, illustrating how the algorithm scales and which games are harder to solve
when_to_show: When discussing PPO's empirical performance on Atari games and the impact of using EnvPool for faster environment simulation
suggested_caption: PPO with EnvPool: episodic return vs. environment steps across Atari games.

description: Learning curves (episodic return vs. wall-clock time) for PPO with Atari EnvPool, showing training speed rather than sample efficiency
teaching_value: Helps students understand the distinction between sample efficiency and wall-clock efficiency, showing how EnvPool accelerates real-time training
when_to_show: When contrasting sample efficiency vs. computational efficiency, or when explaining the practical speedup from vectorized environment libraries like EnvPool
suggested_caption: PPO with EnvPool: episodic return vs. wall-clock time across Atari games.

description: Bar chart or comparison plot of Human Normalized Score (HNS) for PPO vs. baseline implementations, likely comparing value function clipping variants
teaching_value: Demonstrates empirically that value function clipping has negligible impact on PPO performance, helping students understand which implementation details matter
when_to_show: When teaching PPO implementation choices, specifically when discussing whether value function clipping is a necessary component of the algorithm
suggested_caption: HNS comparison: PPO with vs. without value function clipping shows no significant difference.

description: Plot comparing sample efficiency and wall-clock time efficiency of PPO (XLA/JAX with EnvPool) against other implementations, possibly including SEEDRL R2D2
teaching_value: Illustrates the combined benefit of JAX/XLA acceleration and EnvPool on both sample and wall-clock efficiency, helping students evaluate hardware-software co-optimization
when_to_show: When explaining how hardware-accelerated RL frameworks (JAX+XLA) compare to standard implementations in terms of real-world training speed
suggested_caption: PPO JAX/XLA+EnvPool sample and wall-clock efficiency vs. baselines across Atari.

description: Learning curves (episodic return vs. environment steps) for PPO implemented with JAX/XLA and EnvPool across Atari games, benchmarked against baselines
teaching_value: Shows students the sample efficiency of the JAX/XLA PPO variant, enabling direct comparison with standard PPO and R2D2 baselines
when_to_show: When comparing different PPO implementations or discussing the effect of JAX/XLA on learning performance (not just speed)
suggested_caption: PPO (JAX/XLA + EnvPool): episodic return vs. environment steps on Atari.

description: Learning curves (episodic return vs. wall-clock time) for PPO with JAX/XLA and EnvPool, showing real-time training speed across Atari games
teaching_value: Demonstrates the wall-clock speedup achieved by combining JAX/XLA with EnvPool, helping students appreciate the practical impact of hardware-optimized RL
when_to_show: When discussing the practical training time advantages of JAX/XLA-based PPO over standard PyTorch implementations
suggested_caption: PPO (JAX/XLA + EnvPool): episodic return vs. wall-clock time on Atari.

description: Median Human Normalized Score (HNS) comparison chart between CleanRL's PPO (JAX/XLA + EnvPool) and SEEDRL's R2D2 across 57 Atari games
teaching_value: Provides a high-level aggregate performance comparison between an on-policy method (PPO) and a highly optimized off-policy method (R2D2), helping students contextualize PPO's competitiveness
when_to_show: When discussing how PPO compares to state-of-the-art off-policy algorithms at scale, or when introducing the HNS metric for aggregate Atari evaluation
suggested_caption: Median HNS: CleanRL PPO (JAX/XLA) vs. SEEDRL R2D2 across 57 Atari games.

description: Learning curves (episodic return vs. environment steps) for PPO with JAX/XLA scan variant, comparing performance across contributors with different hardware
teaching_value: Illustrates how the scan-based JAX variant achieves similar training performance to the standard XLA variant while reducing compilation time, and highlights hardware variability effects
when_to_show: When explaining JAX-specific optimizations like lax.scan and their trade-offs between compilation time and runtime performance
suggested_caption: PPO JAX scan variant: learning curves showing hardware-dependent speed differences.

description: Learning curves (episodic return vs. wall-clock time) for PPO with JAX/XLA scan variant, highlighting hardware-dependent speed differences between contributors
teaching_value: Shows students that wall-clock performance is hardware-dependent and that the scan variant reduces compilation overhead without sacrificing training speed
when_to_show: When discussing the practical considerations of JAX compilation costs and how lax.scan reduces them in RL training loops
suggested_caption: PPO JAX scan variant: wall-clock learning curves with hardware variability noted.

description: Comparison plot of PPO performance on ProcGen environments, likely showing episodic return across multiple procedurally generated games
teaching_value: Demonstrates PPO's generalization performance on ProcGen, helping students understand how PPO behaves in environments designed to test generalization rather than memorization
when_to_show: When introducing ProcGen as a generalization benchmark and comparing PPO's performance to baselines in non-Atari settings
suggested_caption: PPO on ProcGen environments: performance comparison across procedurally generated games.

description: A learning curve chart comparing training wall-clock time across multiple PPO variants (ppo_atari_envpool_xla_jax.py and related), with runs color-coded by contributor (red for costa-huang, blue/orange for 51616) to highlight hardware-induced speed differences.
teaching_value: Demonstrates that hardware differences between contributors cause measurable speed variation, teaching students to control for hardware when benchmarking RL algorithms and to interpret learning curves with hardware context in mind.
when_to_show: When discussing the impact of hardware on RL training speed, or when comparing JAX-based vs. standard PPO implementations in terms of compilation overhead.
suggested_caption: Training time comparison across PPO variants; color indicates contributor hardware. (CleanRL)

description: A bar or line chart showing average episodic returns for ppo_procgen.py across multiple Procgen environments (easy distribution), compared against OpenAI Baselines' PPO implementation.
teaching_value: Allows students to validate CleanRL's PPO implementation against a trusted baseline, illustrating how to benchmark RL algorithms for correctness and showing expected performance levels on Procgen tasks.
when_to_show: When teaching how to verify RL implementation correctness by comparing against established baselines, or when introducing Procgen as a generalization benchmark.
suggested_caption: Average episodic returns for ppo_procgen.py vs. OpenAI Baselines on Procgen (easy). (CleanRL)

description: A chart showing average episodic returns for ppo_procgen.py plotted against wall-clock time rather than environment steps, compared against OpenAI Baselines' PPO on Procgen easy distribution.
teaching_value: Teaches students the distinction between sample efficiency (steps-based) and wall-time efficiency (time-based) when evaluating RL algorithms, showing how the same performance can look different depending on the x-axis metric.
when_to_show: When explaining the difference between sample efficiency and computational efficiency, or when students need to understand why both time-based and step-based plots are reported.
suggested_caption: Episodic returns vs. wall-clock time for ppo_procgen.py on Procgen easy. (CleanRL)

description: A learning curve chart (episodic return vs. environment steps) comparing ppo_atari_multigpu.py against ppo_atari.py across Atari games, demonstrating equivalent sample efficiency between the single-GPU and multi-GPU implementations.
teaching_value: Shows students that distributing PPO training across multiple GPUs does not degrade sample efficiency, illustrating the principle that parallelism can improve wall-time speed without sacrificing learning quality.
when_to_show: When introducing multi-GPU RL training or discussing how to scale PPO without losing sample efficiency; useful for comparing single vs. multi-GPU setups.
suggested_caption: ppo_atari_multigpu.py matches ppo_atari.py sample efficiency across Atari games. (CleanRL)

description: A wall-clock time learning curve comparing ppo_atari_multigpu.py against ppo_atari.py, showing that the multi-GPU variant achieves approximately 30% faster training on the same hardware.
teaching_value: Quantifies the wall-time speedup from multi-GPU training (~30% faster), helping students understand the practical benefit of distributed training and how to measure training throughput improvements.
when_to_show: When discussing the computational benefits of multi-GPU RL training, or when students ask how much speedup can be expected from parallelizing PPO across GPUs.
suggested_caption: Multi-GPU PPO achieves ~30% wall-time speedup over single-GPU on Atari. (CleanRL)

description: A learning curve for the Breakout Atari game comparing ppo_atari_multigpu.py, ppo_atari.py, and ppo_atari_envpool.py by episodic return vs. environment steps, highlighting sample efficiency differences across implementations.
teaching_value: Illustrates that while multi-GPU training improves wall-time speed, EnvPool-based PPO achieves even greater efficiency, teaching students about the different bottlenecks (environment simulation vs. GPU compute) in RL pipelines.
when_to_show: When comparing different PPO optimization strategies (multi-GPU vs. vectorized environments), or when teaching students about environment simulation as a bottleneck in RL training.
suggested_caption: Breakout episodic returns: ppo_atari_multigpu vs. ppo_atari vs. ppo_atari_envpool. (CleanRL)

description: A wall-clock time learning curve for Breakout comparing ppo_atari_multigpu.py, ppo_atari.py, and ppo_atari_envpool.py, showing that EnvPool-based PPO is faster in wall time despite multi-GPU PPO being 30% faster than single-GPU PPO.
teaching_value: Demonstrates that environment simulation speed (EnvPool) can be a larger bottleneck than GPU compute, teaching students to identify and address the correct bottleneck when optimizing RL training pipelines.
when_to_show: When teaching about performance bottlenecks in RL systems, specifically the trade-off between faster GPU computation and faster environment simulation.
suggested_caption: Breakout wall-time: EnvPool PPO outpaces multi-GPU PPO despite 30% GPU speedup. (CleanRL)

description: A learning curve showing average episodic length (not return) for the Pong_v3 multi-agent Atari environment using ppo_pettingzoo_ma_atari.py, compared against ppo_atari.py, where longer episodes indicate better agent skill.
teaching_value: Introduces episodic length as an alternative performance metric for multi-agent environments where reward signals are sparse or ambiguous, teaching students to select appropriate evaluation metrics for different RL settings.
when_to_show: When introducing multi-agent RL evaluation, or when discussing why episodic return may not always be the best metric and how episodic length can serve as a proxy for skill.
suggested_caption: Episodic length in Pong_v3 as a skill proxy for multi-agent PPO. (CleanRL)
