description: A circular diagram showing the agent-environment interaction loop: the agent receives observations and rewards from the environment, then sends actions back to the environment, forming a closed feedback cycle.
teaching_value: Visually anchors the core RL feedback loop, helping students understand how agent and environment are coupled through observations, actions, and rewards over time.
when_to_show: Show at the very start of any RL introduction, before defining individual terms, to give students a mental model of the overall system.
suggested_caption: Agent-environment interaction loop in reinforcement learning. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: Inline mathematical symbol 's', representing the state variable in reinforcement learning notation.
teaching_value: Introduces the formal notation for 'state' used throughout RL literature, helping students recognize and use standard symbols.
when_to_show: Show when first defining the distinction between states and observations, to establish notation before deeper discussion.
suggested_caption: Symbol 's' denotes the environment state in RL. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: Inline mathematical symbol 'o', representing the observation variable in reinforcement learning notation.
teaching_value: Establishes the formal notation for 'observation', helping students distinguish between full state and partial observation in RL problems.
when_to_show: Show alongside the state symbol when contrasting fully observable vs. partially observable environments.
suggested_caption: Symbol 'o' denotes the agent's observation in RL. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: Inline mathematical symbol 'μ' (mu), used to denote a deterministic policy in reinforcement learning.
teaching_value: Introduces the standard notation for deterministic policies, allowing students to read and write RL equations correctly.
when_to_show: Show when introducing the concept of policies, specifically when distinguishing deterministic from stochastic policies.
suggested_caption: Symbol 'μ' denotes a deterministic policy in RL. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: Mathematical equation 'a_t = μ(s_t)', expressing that at time t the action is a deterministic function of the current state.
teaching_value: Formalizes the deterministic policy concept: the action is fully determined by the state, with no randomness, helping students understand the mathematical structure of deterministic decision-making.
when_to_show: Show when formally defining deterministic policies, immediately before or after introducing the stochastic policy equation for contrast.
suggested_caption: Deterministic policy: action at time t is a function of state. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: Inline mathematical symbol 'π' (pi), the standard notation for a stochastic policy in reinforcement learning.
teaching_value: Establishes the canonical symbol for stochastic policies, which is ubiquitous in RL literature and essential for reading policy gradient and other algorithm derivations.
when_to_show: Show when introducing stochastic policies, contrasting with the deterministic 'μ' notation.
suggested_caption: Symbol 'π' denotes a stochastic policy in RL. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: Mathematical equation 'a_t ~ π(· | s_t)', expressing that at time t the action is sampled from a conditional probability distribution given the current state.
teaching_value: Formalizes the stochastic policy: the agent samples an action from a distribution conditioned on the state, illustrating the probabilistic nature of stochastic decision-making.
when_to_show: Show directly after the deterministic policy equation to highlight the contrast between deterministic and stochastic action selection.
suggested_caption: Stochastic policy: action sampled from distribution given state. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: Inline mathematical symbol 'θ' (theta), representing the parameters (e.g., neural network weights) of a parameterized policy.
teaching_value: Introduces the notation for policy parameters, which is central to understanding how deep RL optimizes policies via gradient-based methods.
when_to_show: Show when transitioning from abstract policies to parameterized (deep learning) policies, before presenting the subscript notation.
suggested_caption: Symbol 'θ' denotes learnable parameters of a policy. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: Inline mathematical symbol 'φ' (phi), an alternative notation for policy parameters in parameterized policies.
teaching_value: Shows that policy parameters may be denoted by either θ or φ depending on context, helping students recognize both conventions in the literature.
when_to_show: Show alongside θ when discussing parameterized policies to prevent confusion when students encounter different notation in papers.
suggested_caption: Symbol 'φ' is an alternative notation for policy parameters. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: A two-line mathematical display showing both parameterized policy forms: 'a_t = μ_θ(s_t)' for deterministic and 'a_t ~ π_θ(· | s_t)' for stochastic, with θ as a subscript denoting parameters.
teaching_value: Unifies the deterministic and stochastic policy equations under the parameterized framework, showing students how neural network parameters θ are incorporated into both policy types simultaneously.
when_to_show: Show after introducing both policy types and the parameter notation, as a synthesis that connects all prior notation into the deep RL setting.
suggested_caption: Parameterized deterministic and stochastic policies with parameters θ. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: The tanh activation function symbol (\tanh), used as the activation function in the MLP policy network with two hidden layers of size 64
teaching_value: Identifies the specific nonlinearity used in a deterministic policy network, helping students understand the architectural choice of tanh over alternatives like ReLU for policy networks
when_to_show: When introducing deterministic policies and explaining how neural networks map observations to actions; pair with the MLP code snippet discussion
suggested_caption: tanh activation used in deterministic MLP policy network. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: The log-likelihood expression \log \pi_{\theta}(a|s), representing the log probability of action a given state s under a parameterized stochastic policy
teaching_value: Introduces the log-likelihood notation central to policy gradient methods; students must understand this quantity to grasp how policy parameters are optimized
when_to_show: When transitioning from deterministic to stochastic policies, and before deriving policy gradient objectives that require computing log-likelihoods
suggested_caption: Log-likelihood of action a under stochastic policy \pi_\theta. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: The probability vector symbol P_{\theta}(s), denoting the output probability distribution over discrete actions from a categorical policy network given state s
teaching_value: Clarifies the notation for the final softmax output of a categorical policy, helping students connect the neural network output to a proper probability distribution over actions
when_to_show: When explaining categorical policies and how the neural network output is converted to action probabilities via softmax; use before the log-likelihood formula
suggested_caption: P_\theta(s): probability vector over discrete actions from categorical policy. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: The symbol 'a' representing a discrete action, used as an index into the probability vector for computing log-likelihood in a categorical policy
teaching_value: Reinforces that in discrete action spaces, actions serve as indices into the probability vector, which is key to understanding how log-likelihoods are computed for categorical policies
when_to_show: When explaining how to compute the log-likelihood of a specific action under a categorical policy, just before presenting the indexing formula
suggested_caption: Action 'a' used as index into categorical probability vector. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: The complete log-likelihood formula for a categorical policy: \log \pi_{\theta}(a|s) = \log [P_{\theta}(s)]_a, showing that the log-likelihood equals the log of the a-th entry of the probability vector
teaching_value: Provides the concrete, implementable formula for computing log-likelihoods in categorical policies, directly connecting abstract notation to a simple vector indexing operation students can code
when_to_show: After introducing P_\theta(s) and the action index 'a'; show this as the key formula students need to implement log-likelihood computation for policy gradient algorithms
suggested_caption: Log-likelihood for categorical policy via probability vector indexing. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: The covariance matrix symbol \Sigma, representing the covariance matrix of a multivariate Gaussian distribution used in diagonal Gaussian policies
teaching_value: Introduces the covariance matrix notation in the context of Gaussian policies, helping students understand what is being simplified when moving to a diagonal Gaussian
when_to_show: When introducing diagonal Gaussian policies; use to contrast the full covariance matrix with the diagonal simplification that makes the policy tractable
suggested_caption: Covariance matrix \Sigma of multivariate Gaussian policy distribution. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: The mean action function \mu_{\theta}(s), representing the neural network output mapping observations to mean actions in a diagonal Gaussian policy
teaching_value: Identifies the core neural network component of a diagonal Gaussian policy, showing students that the mean of the action distribution is state-dependent and learned
when_to_show: When explaining the architecture of diagonal Gaussian policies; contrast with the two approaches for representing the standard deviation
suggested_caption: \mu_\theta(s): neural network mapping observations to mean actions. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: The log standard deviation symbol \log \sigma, representing the parameterization choice for the spread of a diagonal Gaussian policy
teaching_value: Explains why log standard deviations are used instead of standard deviations directly — they are unconstrained real-valued parameters, making optimization easier
when_to_show: When discussing why log stds are preferred over raw stds in Gaussian policies; emphasize the unconstrained optimization benefit
suggested_caption: Log standard deviation \log\sigma: unconstrained parameterization for Gaussian policy. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: The state-dependent log standard deviation function \log \sigma_{\theta}(s), representing the second approach where a neural network maps states to log standard deviations
teaching_value: Contrasts with the state-independent log std approach, showing students that the policy variance can itself be learned as a function of state for more expressive policies
when_to_show: When comparing the two approaches to representing covariance in Gaussian policies; highlight the trade-off between simplicity (fixed log std) and expressiveness (state-dependent log std)
suggested_caption: \log\sigma_\theta(s): state-dependent log std from a neural network. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: The interval (-\infty, \infty), indicating that log standard deviations are unconstrained and can take any real value, unlike standard deviations which must be non-negative
teaching_value: Concretely illustrates the mathematical reason for using log parameterization: removing the non-negativity constraint simplifies gradient-based optimization of policy parameters
when_to_show: When justifying the use of log standard deviations; show alongside the \log\sigma notation to explain why this parameterization is preferred in practice
suggested_caption: Log stds range over (-\infty, \infty), enabling unconstrained optimization. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: Mathematical notation for the state-dependent standard deviation function sigma_theta(s), output by a neural network parameterized by theta
teaching_value: Clarifies that the standard deviation in a diagonal Gaussian policy is a learned, state-dependent function — not a fixed constant — helping students understand expressive stochastic policies
when_to_show: When introducing how neural networks output distribution parameters for continuous action spaces; pair with the mean network mu_theta(s) for contrast
suggested_caption: State-dependent standard deviation output by policy network. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: Single variable 'z' representing a noise vector sampled from a spherical Gaussian, used in the reparameterization trick for action sampling
teaching_value: Identifies the noise variable in the reparameterization trick, helping students understand how stochastic sampling is decomposed into a deterministic transform plus independent noise
when_to_show: When explaining how actions are sampled from a Gaussian policy using the reparameterization trick; show alongside the full sampling equation
suggested_caption: Noise vector z used in reparameterized action sampling. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: Mathematical expression z ~ N(0, I), indicating that the noise vector z is sampled from a standard spherical Gaussian distribution
teaching_value: Shows the exact distribution from which noise is drawn in the reparameterization trick, grounding the sampling procedure in standard probability notation
when_to_show: When teaching the reparameterization trick for Gaussian policies; use to explain why sampling z from N(0,I) and transforming it is equivalent to sampling directly from N(mu, sigma)
suggested_caption: Noise z drawn from standard spherical Gaussian N(0, I). (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: Action sampling equation: a = mu_theta(s) + sigma_theta(s) ⊙ z, showing how an action is computed as the mean plus elementwise-scaled noise
teaching_value: Concisely encodes the reparameterization trick for diagonal Gaussian policies, showing students how stochastic actions are generated in a differentiable way
when_to_show: When teaching action sampling from Gaussian policies; use as the central equation to explain how mean and standard deviation combine with noise to produce actions
suggested_caption: Action sampled via reparameterization: mean plus scaled noise. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: The Hadamard (elementwise) product symbol ⊙, used to denote elementwise multiplication of two vectors
teaching_value: Defines the ⊙ operator used in the action sampling equation, preventing confusion between elementwise and matrix multiplication in the policy formula
when_to_show: When first introducing the action sampling equation a = mu + sigma ⊙ z; clarify this symbol before students encounter the full formula
suggested_caption: ⊙ denotes elementwise (Hadamard) product of two vectors. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: Variable 'k' representing the dimensionality of the action space in the log-likelihood formula for a diagonal Gaussian policy
teaching_value: Contextualizes the log-likelihood formula by identifying k as the action dimension, helping students understand how the formula scales with action space size
when_to_show: When introducing the log-likelihood formula for diagonal Gaussian policies; clarify k before presenting the full summation formula
suggested_caption: k denotes the dimensionality of the action vector. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: Expression mu = mu_theta(s), defining the mean action as the output of a neural network policy evaluated at state s
teaching_value: Establishes the shorthand notation for the policy mean used in the log-likelihood formula, connecting the neural network output to the Gaussian distribution parameter
when_to_show: When presenting the log-likelihood formula; use to remind students that mu is not a fixed value but a state-dependent neural network output
suggested_caption: Policy mean mu as neural network output at state s. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: Expression sigma = sigma_theta(s), defining the standard deviation as the output of a neural network policy evaluated at state s
teaching_value: Establishes the shorthand for the policy standard deviation used in the log-likelihood formula, reinforcing that both mean and variance are learned functions of state
when_to_show: When presenting the log-likelihood formula alongside mu = mu_theta(s); emphasize that sigma is also state-dependent and learned
suggested_caption: Policy standard deviation sigma as neural network output at state s. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: Full log-likelihood formula for a k-dimensional action under a diagonal Gaussian policy: log pi_theta(a|s) = -1/2 * (sum over i of [(a_i - mu_i)^2 / sigma_i^2 + 2*log(sigma_i)] + k*log(2*pi))
teaching_value: Provides the exact formula needed to compute policy log-probabilities for policy gradient methods, showing students how each action dimension contributes independently and how the normalization constant scales with dimensionality
when_to_show: When teaching policy gradient algorithms that require log-probabilities (e.g., REINFORCE, PPO); use to show how to implement log pi in code and why diagonal Gaussian is computationally tractable
suggested_caption: Log-likelihood of action under diagonal Gaussian policy. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: Greek letter tau (τ) representing a trajectory — a sequence of states and actions — in reinforcement learning notation
teaching_value: Introduces the standard notation for trajectories, which is foundational for understanding return calculations, policy gradient objectives, and trajectory-based RL algorithms
when_to_show: When first introducing the concept of trajectories in RL; use as a reference symbol before presenting trajectory return formulas or policy gradient objectives
suggested_caption: τ (tau) denotes a trajectory of states and actions. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: LaTeX-rendered equation defining a trajectory τ as an ordered sequence of alternating states and actions: (s_0, a_0, s_1, a_1, ...)
teaching_value: Gives students the precise mathematical notation for a trajectory, making explicit that it interleaves states and actions over time
when_to_show: Show at the very start of introducing trajectories, before discussing transitions or policies, to anchor the formal definition
suggested_caption: A trajectory τ: alternating states and actions over time. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: LaTeX-rendered symbol s_0, representing the initial state of the environment at time step zero
teaching_value: Isolates the notation for the initial state, helping students recognize it as a distinct, specially-sampled element of a trajectory
when_to_show: Show when explaining that the first state in a trajectory is sampled from a start-state distribution, to connect symbol to concept
suggested_caption: s₀: the initial state, sampled from the start distribution. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: LaTeX-rendered symbol ρ_0 (rho subscript 0), denoting the start-state distribution from which the initial state is sampled
teaching_value: Introduces the standard notation for the start-state distribution, a key probabilistic concept in RL problem formulation
when_to_show: Show alongside s_0 when explaining how episodes begin, to clarify that the initial state is stochastic
suggested_caption: ρ₀: the start-state distribution over initial environment states. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: LaTeX-rendered equation s_0 ~ ρ_0(·), expressing that the initial state is drawn by sampling from the start-state distribution
teaching_value: Shows the complete sampling statement in standard probabilistic notation, teaching students how to read and write stochastic initialization formally
when_to_show: Show immediately after introducing ρ_0 and s_0 separately, to unify them into the full initialization equation
suggested_caption: Initial state s₀ is sampled from start distribution ρ₀. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: LaTeX-rendered symbol t, representing a discrete time step index in a trajectory
teaching_value: Establishes the time-step index notation used throughout RL, helping students parse time-indexed expressions like s_t and a_t
when_to_show: Show when first introducing state transitions to clarify the indexing convention before presenting s_t and s_{t+1}
suggested_caption: t: discrete time step index in a trajectory. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: LaTeX-rendered symbol s_t, representing the environment state at time step t
teaching_value: Provides the standard notation for a state at an arbitrary time step, essential for reading transition and policy equations
when_to_show: Show when explaining state transitions, pairing it with s_{t+1} to illustrate the before/after relationship
suggested_caption: s_t: the environment state at time step t. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: LaTeX-rendered expression t+1, representing the next discrete time step after t
teaching_value: Clarifies the successor time index, helping students understand the one-step-ahead relationship central to Markov transitions
when_to_show: Show alongside t when introducing the concept of state transitions to make the temporal relationship explicit
suggested_caption: t+1: the successor time step following t. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: LaTeX-rendered symbol s_{t+1}, representing the next environment state after taking action a_t in state s_t
teaching_value: Introduces the notation for the successor state, a core element in expressing both deterministic and stochastic transition dynamics
when_to_show: Show when presenting transition dynamics equations, paired with s_t and a_t to illustrate the full transition tuple
suggested_caption: s_{t+1}: the next state after action a_t is taken. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: LaTeX-rendered symbol a_t, representing the action taken by the agent at time step t
teaching_value: Establishes the standard notation for a time-indexed action, necessary for understanding policy definitions and transition equations
when_to_show: Show when introducing how agents interact with the environment, before presenting transition or policy equations
suggested_caption: a_t: the agent's action at time step t. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: LaTeX-rendered equation s_{t+1} = f(s_t, a_t), expressing deterministic state transition dynamics as a function of current state and action
teaching_value: Concisely formalizes deterministic transitions, showing students that the next state is fully determined by the current state and action — embodying the Markov property
when_to_show: Show when contrasting deterministic vs. stochastic transitions, as the baseline case before introducing probabilistic transition models
suggested_caption: Deterministic transition: next state is f(s_t, a_t). (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: Mathematical equation showing state transition dynamics: s_{t+1} sampled from conditional probability distribution P given current state s_t and action a_t
teaching_value: Shows students the stochastic nature of environment transitions — the next state is not deterministic but drawn from a probability distribution conditioned on current state and action
when_to_show: When introducing how trajectories are generated step-by-step; use to contrast deterministic vs stochastic environments
suggested_caption: State transition: next state sampled from environment dynamics P. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: Single symbol 'R' representing the reward function notation used throughout the reward and return section
teaching_value: Introduces the symbolic notation for the reward function before its formal definition, helping students recognize R as the reward signal in subsequent equations
when_to_show: When first introducing the reward function concept; use as a reference anchor before showing the full reward function definitions
suggested_caption: R denotes the reward function — central to RL objective. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: Equation defining reward r_t as a function R of current state s_t, action a_t, and next state s_{t+1} — the most general form of the reward function
teaching_value: Shows students the full general form of the reward function depending on state, action, AND next state, establishing the most expressive reward formulation before introducing simplifications
when_to_show: When introducing reward functions; present this as the general case before showing the simplified forms r_t=R(s_t) and r_t=R(s_t,a_t)
suggested_caption: General reward function depends on state, action, and next state. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: Equation showing simplified reward function r_t = R(s_t) — reward depends only on the current state
teaching_value: Illustrates the simplest reward formulation where only the current state matters, helping students understand that reward function complexity can vary and this simplification is common in practice
when_to_show: After presenting the general reward form; use to show the state-only simplification and discuss when this assumption is reasonable
suggested_caption: Simplified reward: depends only on current state s_t. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: Equation showing reward function r_t = R(s_t, a_t) — reward depends on current state and action taken, but not the next state
teaching_value: Presents the intermediate reward formulation (state-action pair), helping students understand the spectrum of reward function complexity between state-only and full state-action-next-state forms
when_to_show: When comparing reward function variants; place between the state-only and full forms to show the progression of complexity
suggested_caption: Reward as function of state-action pair — common simplification. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: Notation R(τ) representing the return (cumulative reward) along a trajectory τ — the quantity the agent seeks to maximize
teaching_value: Introduces the critical notation for trajectory return, bridging per-step rewards to the overall optimization objective; students need this symbol to understand the RL objective
when_to_show: When transitioning from per-step reward r_t to cumulative return; use to set up both finite-horizon and infinite-horizon return formulas
suggested_caption: R(τ) denotes cumulative return over trajectory τ. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: Equation for finite-horizon undiscounted return: R(τ) = sum of r_t from t=0 to T — simple sum of rewards over a fixed time window
teaching_value: Gives students the concrete mathematical definition of finite-horizon return, showing that it is simply an unweighted sum of rewards up to a terminal step T, making the optimization objective tangible
when_to_show: When introducing return types; present first as the simpler case before the discounted infinite-horizon version to build intuition
suggested_caption: Finite-horizon return: simple sum of rewards over T steps. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: Notation showing discount factor γ (gamma) constrained to the open interval (0,1) — a key hyperparameter in infinite-horizon RL
teaching_value: Defines the valid range of the discount factor, helping students understand why γ must be strictly between 0 and 1 for mathematical convergence of the infinite sum and to encode time preference
when_to_show: When introducing infinite-horizon discounted return; use to explain why γ<1 is necessary for convergence before showing the full discounted return formula
suggested_caption: Discount factor γ ∈ (0,1) ensures convergence of infinite return. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: Equation for infinite-horizon discounted return: R(τ) = sum from t=0 to infinity of γ^t * r_t — rewards weighted by exponentially decaying discount factor
teaching_value: Shows students how the discount factor γ^t exponentially downweights future rewards, making the infinite sum mathematically tractable and encoding the intuition that near-term rewards are more valuable
when_to_show: After introducing γ and finite-horizon return; use to show how discounting solves the infinite-sum convergence problem and discuss the cash-now-vs-later intuition
suggested_caption: Infinite-horizon discounted return: future rewards weighted by γ^t. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: Symbol T representing the time horizon (terminal timestep) in finite-horizon return formulations
teaching_value: Clarifies the notation for the finite time horizon used in episodic RL settings, helping students distinguish between finite-horizon (episodic) and infinite-horizon (continuing) task formulations
when_to_show: When discussing the RL problem setup and expected return; use to clarify the distinction between episodic (finite T) and continuing (infinite horizon) tasks
suggested_caption: T denotes the finite time horizon in episodic RL tasks. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: Mathematical formula for the probability of a trajectory τ given policy π, expressed as the product of the initial state distribution and the product over timesteps of transition probabilities and policy probabilities.
teaching_value: Shows students exactly how trajectory probability decomposes into initial state distribution, environment dynamics, and policy choices — making the chain rule of probability concrete in the RL setting.
when_to_show: When introducing how trajectories are distributed under a policy; use before deriving the expected return to ground the expectation in a concrete probability measure.
suggested_caption: Trajectory probability factored over initial state, transitions, and policy. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: Inline mathematical symbol J(π), denoting the expected return of policy π — the objective function in reinforcement learning.
teaching_value: Introduces the standard notation for the RL objective, helping students recognize J(π) as the quantity to be maximized throughout RL literature.
when_to_show: When first defining the RL optimization objective; pair with the full definition of J(π) to anchor the notation before showing the optimization problem.
suggested_caption: J(π): the expected return objective for policy π. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: Full definition of J(π) as the integral of trajectory probability times trajectory return, equivalently written as the expectation of R(τ) under trajectories sampled from π.
teaching_value: Bridges the trajectory probability formula and the optimization goal by showing how expected return is computed as a weighted average of returns over all possible trajectories.
when_to_show: Immediately after presenting the trajectory probability formula; use to show how integrating reward over the trajectory distribution yields the scalar objective J(π).
suggested_caption: Expected return J(π) as an integral over trajectory probabilities and returns. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: The core RL optimization problem: π* equals the argmax over all policies π of J(π), defining the optimal policy as the one maximizing expected return.
teaching_value: Crystallizes the entire goal of RL into a single equation, showing students that all RL algorithms are ultimately solving this argmax problem.
when_to_show: After defining J(π); use as the capstone of the RL problem formulation section to unify trajectory probability, expected return, and the optimization goal.
suggested_caption: The RL optimization problem: find π* that maximizes expected return J(π). (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: Inline symbol π* denoting the optimal policy — the policy that achieves maximum expected return.
teaching_value: Establishes the notation for the optimal policy, which recurs throughout RL theory and algorithm descriptions.
when_to_show: When referencing the optimal policy in context; useful as a quick notation reminder when students encounter π* in value function definitions or Bellman optimality equations.
suggested_caption: π*: notation for the optimal policy in RL. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: Inline symbol V^π(s) denoting the on-policy value function — the expected return starting from state s and following policy π.
teaching_value: Introduces the standard notation for the state value function, a foundational concept used in nearly every RL algorithm.
when_to_show: When introducing value functions; pair with the full definition equation to help students connect the symbol to its mathematical meaning.
suggested_caption: V^π(s): on-policy state value function notation. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: Full definition of the on-policy value function V^π(s) as the expected return of trajectories sampled from policy π, conditioned on starting in state s.
teaching_value: Makes precise what 'value of a state' means mathematically — an expectation over future trajectories — helping students understand how value functions encode long-term reward.
when_to_show: When formally defining value functions; use to show students how the expectation is conditioned on the initial state, distinguishing V^π from J(π).
suggested_caption: V^π(s): expected return from state s following policy π. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: Inline symbol Q^π(s,a) denoting the on-policy action-value (Q) function — expected return starting from state s, taking action a, then following policy π.
teaching_value: Introduces Q-function notation, which is central to Q-learning, actor-critic methods, and policy gradient algorithms.
when_to_show: When introducing the Q-function; pair with its full definition to help students distinguish it from V^π(s) by the additional action argument.
suggested_caption: Q^π(s,a): on-policy action-value function notation. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: Full definition of the on-policy action-value function Q^π(s,a) as the expected return conditioned on starting in state s, taking action a, then following policy π forever after.
teaching_value: Clarifies the key distinction between V^π and Q^π: Q^π allows an arbitrary first action before reverting to policy π, which is essential for understanding policy improvement and advantage functions.
when_to_show: After defining V^π(s); use to highlight the difference introduced by conditioning on both state and action, and to motivate why Q-functions are useful for action selection.
suggested_caption: Q^π(s,a): expected return from state s, action a, then policy π. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: Inline symbol V*(s) denoting the optimal value function — the maximum expected return achievable from state s under the best possible policy.
teaching_value: Introduces the notation for the optimal value function, which appears in Bellman optimality equations and is the target of many RL algorithms.
when_to_show: When transitioning from on-policy value functions to optimal value functions; use to set up the Bellman optimality equations and the relationship between V* and Q*.
suggested_caption: V*(s): optimal state value function — best achievable expected return. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: Mathematical equation defining the Optimal Value Function V*(s) as the maximum over all policies of the expected return R(τ) given that the trajectory starts in state s
teaching_value: Gives students the precise formal definition of V*(s), showing that optimality is achieved by maximizing over all possible policies, anchoring the concept of 'best possible expected return from a state'
when_to_show: Introduce when first defining optimal value functions, immediately after explaining on-policy value functions V^π(s), to contrast the role of the max operator
suggested_caption: Optimal value function V*(s): max expected return over all policies. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: Inline notation Q*(s,a) representing the Optimal Action-Value Function symbol used as a reference label in the surrounding text
teaching_value: Establishes the symbolic notation Q*(s,a) for students, helping them recognize the optimal action-value function in equations and discussions throughout the article
when_to_show: Show when introducing the Q* notation for the first time, before presenting its full definition, to help students associate the symbol with its meaning
suggested_caption: Notation for the optimal action-value (Q*) function. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: Mathematical equation defining the Optimal Action-Value Function Q*(s,a) as the maximum over all policies of expected return R(τ), conditioned on starting in state s and taking action a at time 0
teaching_value: Clarifies that Q*(s,a) fixes the first action arbitrarily and then assumes optimal behavior thereafter, helping students understand the distinction between V* and Q* and the role of the initial action
when_to_show: Present directly after V*(s) definition to highlight the structural difference: Q* conditions on both state and initial action, while V* only conditions on state
suggested_caption: Optimal Q*(s,a): max expected return fixing initial action a. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: Mathematical equation showing that the on-policy value function V^π(s) equals the expectation over actions drawn from policy π of the action-value function Q^π(s,a)
teaching_value: Demonstrates the key relationship between V^π and Q^π, showing students that the state value is simply the policy-weighted average of action values, reinforcing how the two functions are connected
when_to_show: Use when teaching the connections between value functions; ask students to derive this from definitions before revealing the equation as confirmation
suggested_caption: V^π(s) as policy-weighted expectation of Q^π(s,a). (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: Mathematical equation showing that the optimal value function V*(s) equals the maximum over actions a of the optimal action-value function Q*(s,a)
teaching_value: Shows students the critical link between V* and Q*: the best state value is achieved by picking the best action, bridging the two optimal value functions and motivating greedy action selection
when_to_show: Present alongside the V^π–Q^π relationship to contrast on-policy averaging with optimal maximization; use to motivate the concept of greedy action selection
suggested_caption: V*(s) equals max over actions of Q*(s,a). (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: Inline symbol Q* representing the optimal action-value function, used as a reference in the surrounding text about the connection between Q* and the optimal policy
teaching_value: Reinforces the Q* notation in context of the optimal policy discussion, helping students track the symbol as it appears in the argument about action selection
when_to_show: Surface when discussing the relationship between Q* and optimal action selection, as a notation anchor before presenting the argmax formula
suggested_caption: Q*: the optimal action-value function symbol. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: Inline symbol a*(s) representing the optimal action as a function of state, used as a reference label in the surrounding text
teaching_value: Establishes the notation a*(s) for the optimal action, helping students recognize that the optimal policy can be expressed as a deterministic function of state when Q* is known
when_to_show: Introduce when explaining that knowing Q* is sufficient to derive the optimal policy, before showing the argmax formula for a*(s)
suggested_caption: Notation a*(s) for the optimal action at state s. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: Mathematical equation defining the optimal action a*(s) as the argmax over actions a of the optimal action-value function Q*(s,a)
teaching_value: Shows students concretely how to extract an optimal policy from Q*: simply pick the action with the highest Q-value at each state, demonstrating the practical utility of learning Q*
when_to_show: Present after introducing Q* and V*=max_a Q*(s,a); use to motivate why many RL algorithms focus on learning Q* rather than the policy directly
suggested_caption: Optimal action a*(s) = argmax over Q*(s,a). (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: System of two Bellman equations for on-policy value functions: V^π(s) expressed as expected immediate reward plus discounted next-state value, and Q^π(s,a) expressed as expected immediate reward plus discounted expected next action-value
teaching_value: Presents the recursive self-consistency structure of value functions, showing students how current values decompose into immediate reward and future values — the foundation of dynamic programming and TD learning
when_to_show: Introduce after students understand V^π and Q^π definitions; use to explain why value functions can be estimated iteratively and how TD methods work
suggested_caption: Bellman equations for V^π and Q^π: reward plus discounted future value. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: Shorthand notation 's' ~ P indicating that the next state s' is sampled from the environment's transition probability distribution P
teaching_value: Clarifies the probabilistic notation used in Bellman equations, helping students understand that next-state transitions are stochastic and governed by environment dynamics
when_to_show: Show when introducing Bellman equations to explain the notation before students encounter it in the full equations, preventing confusion about what s' ~ P means
suggested_caption: Notation: next state s' sampled from transition distribution P. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: LaTeX-rendered mathematical notation showing s' ~ P(·|s,a), representing that the next state s' is sampled from the environment's transition probability distribution conditioned on current state s and action a.
teaching_value: Clarifies the shorthand notation used in Bellman equations, helping students decode the compact mathematical expressions by explicitly showing the stochastic transition dynamics.
when_to_show: When introducing Bellman equations and a student asks what the expectation subscript means or how the next state is determined.
suggested_caption: Shorthand: next state s' sampled from environment transition rules. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: LaTeX-rendered symbol s', representing the next state variable in the context of Bellman equation notation.
teaching_value: Isolates the next-state variable s' so students can identify it as a distinct symbol from current state s, reinforcing the temporal structure of RL transitions.
when_to_show: When a student is confused about the difference between s (current state) and s' (next state) in Bellman equation derivations.
suggested_caption: s' denotes the next state after a transition. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: LaTeX-rendered shorthand notation a ~ π, indicating that action a is sampled from the policy π.
teaching_value: Shows the compact shorthand for action sampling, helping students understand that actions are drawn stochastically from the policy distribution in on-policy settings.
when_to_show: When explaining on-policy Bellman equations and students need to understand how actions are selected under a given policy.
suggested_caption: Shorthand: action a sampled from policy π. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: LaTeX-rendered full notation a ~ π(·|s), showing that action a is sampled from the policy distribution conditioned on the current state s.
teaching_value: Provides the full expanded form of the action-sampling shorthand, making explicit that the policy is a conditional distribution over actions given the current state.
when_to_show: When a student needs to understand the full meaning behind the shorthand a ~ π in Bellman equations, especially when first encountering policy notation.
suggested_caption: Full form: action a drawn from policy conditioned on state s. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: LaTeX-rendered shorthand notation a' ~ π, indicating that the next action a' is sampled from the policy π.
teaching_value: Introduces the next-action variable a' in the context of Bellman equations, helping students track the two-step lookahead structure (current action a, next action a') in Q-function equations.
when_to_show: When discussing the on-policy Q-function Bellman equation and students need to distinguish between current action a and next action a'.
suggested_caption: Shorthand: next action a' sampled from policy π. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: LaTeX-rendered full notation a' ~ π(·|s'), showing that the next action a' is sampled from the policy conditioned on the next state s'.
teaching_value: Completes the two-step lookahead notation by showing that the next action depends on the next state, reinforcing the Markovian structure and how the policy operates at each step.
when_to_show: When deriving the on-policy Q-function Bellman equation and students need to see the full expanded form of the next-action sampling notation.
suggested_caption: Full form: next action a' drawn from policy conditioned on next state s'. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: LaTeX-rendered system of two Bellman optimality equations: V*(s) = max_a E[r(s,a) + γV*(s')] and Q*(s,a) = E[r(s,a) + γ max_a' Q*(s',a')], defining the optimal value function and optimal action-value function.
teaching_value: Presents the core recursive definitions of optimal value functions, showing students how the max operator encodes optimal decision-making and how V* and Q* relate to each other through the Bellman optimality principle.
when_to_show: When teaching the distinction between on-policy and optimal value functions, or when introducing dynamic programming and Q-learning foundations.
suggested_caption: Bellman optimality equations for V* and Q* with max over actions. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: LaTeX-rendered 'max' operator symbol, highlighting the key mathematical operation that distinguishes optimal Bellman equations from on-policy Bellman equations.
teaching_value: Draws attention to the max operator as the critical difference between on-policy and optimal Bellman equations, helping students understand that optimal policies always select the highest-value action.
when_to_show: When a student asks why optimal Bellman equations look different from on-policy ones, or when explaining why optimal policies are deterministic.
suggested_caption: The 'max' operator distinguishes optimal from on-policy Bellman equations. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: LaTeX-rendered notation A^π(s,a), representing the advantage function for policy π evaluated at state s and action a.
teaching_value: Introduces the formal symbol for the advantage function, helping students connect the notation to its meaning: how much better action a is compared to the average action under policy π in state s.
when_to_show: When introducing the advantage function concept, especially before discussing policy gradient algorithms like A2C or PPO that rely on advantage estimates.
suggested_caption: A^π(s,a): advantage function measuring relative action quality under policy π. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: LaTeX-rendered notation π(·|s), representing the policy as a conditional probability distribution over actions given state s.
teaching_value: Reinforces the formal definition of a stochastic policy as a distribution over actions conditioned on state, which is foundational for understanding both value functions and policy gradient methods.
when_to_show: When introducing the advantage function or policy gradient methods, to remind students that the policy is a full distribution over actions, not just a single action.
suggested_caption: π(·|s): policy as a conditional distribution over actions given state s. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: Mathematical equation defining the advantage function: A^π(s,a) = Q^π(s,a) - V^π(s), showing it as the difference between the action-value function and the state-value function.
teaching_value: Concisely captures the formal definition of advantage, helping students see that advantage is a relative measure — how much better a specific action is compared to the average action under the current policy.
when_to_show: Introduce this equation immediately after explaining Q and V functions, when transitioning to why relative action quality matters more than absolute value in policy gradient methods.
suggested_caption: Advantage function: how much better action a is than average. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: Mathematical notation for the MDP 5-tuple: ⟨S, A, R, P, ρ₀⟩, representing the formal components of a Markov Decision Process.
teaching_value: Gives students the canonical formal shorthand for an MDP, enabling them to read academic literature and understand that all RL environments can be described by these five components.
when_to_show: Show when first introducing the formal MDP framework, as an overview before unpacking each component individually.
suggested_caption: The MDP 5-tuple: the formal foundation of all RL environments. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: Symbol S representing the set of all valid states (state space) in an MDP.
teaching_value: Isolates the state space component of the MDP tuple, helping students associate the symbol S with the complete set of possible environment states.
when_to_show: Show when enumerating each element of the MDP 5-tuple, specifically when defining what the state space represents.
suggested_caption: S: the set of all valid states in an MDP. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: Symbol A representing the set of all valid actions (action space) in an MDP.
teaching_value: Isolates the action space component of the MDP tuple, helping students associate the symbol A with the complete set of actions an agent can take.
when_to_show: Show when enumerating each element of the MDP 5-tuple, specifically when defining what the action space represents.
suggested_caption: A: the set of all valid actions in an MDP. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: Mathematical definition of the reward function R: S × A × S → ℝ, mapping state-action-next-state triples to real-valued rewards.
teaching_value: Shows students the formal type signature of the reward function, clarifying that rewards depend on the current state, action taken, and resulting next state — not just the action alone.
when_to_show: Show when defining the reward component of the MDP, especially to clarify that reward can depend on the transition outcome, not just the action.
suggested_caption: Reward function R maps state-action-next-state triples to real numbers. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: Equation r_t = R(s_t, a_t, s_{t+1}) showing how the reward at timestep t is computed from the current state, action, and next state.
teaching_value: Grounds the abstract reward function definition in concrete timestep notation, helping students understand how rewards are computed during an actual trajectory.
when_to_show: Show alongside the reward function definition (Image 5) to connect the abstract mapping to its practical use during environment interaction.
suggested_caption: Reward at time t depends on current state, action, and next state. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: Mathematical definition of the transition probability function P: S × A → 𝒫(S), mapping state-action pairs to probability distributions over next states.
teaching_value: Formalizes the stochastic nature of environment dynamics, showing students that transitions are probability distributions — not deterministic outcomes — which is central to understanding RL.
when_to_show: Show when explaining environment dynamics and the Markov property, to illustrate that the next state is probabilistically determined by only the current state and action.
suggested_caption: Transition function P maps state-action pairs to distributions over next states. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: Conditional probability notation P(s'|s,a) representing the probability of transitioning to next state s' given current state s and action a.
teaching_value: Provides the shorthand conditional probability form of the transition function, which is the notation most commonly encountered in RL literature and algorithms.
when_to_show: Show alongside Image 7 as the practical shorthand notation, and when discussing the Markov property to reinforce that history beyond (s,a) is irrelevant.
suggested_caption: P(s'|s,a): probability of reaching s' from state s with action a. (OpenAI Spinning Up)

description: A 2D grid world environment called 'CliffWorld' showing a grid with a starting state, goal state, cliff tiles, and navigable tiles. The agent must traverse the grid from start to goal while avoiding falling off the cliff.
teaching_value: Concretely illustrates the multi-state, delayed-reward problem setting that motivates Q-learning over bandits. Students can visually map abstract RL concepts (states, actions, rewards, obstacles) onto a tangible spatial layout.
when_to_show: Show when introducing the transition from single-state bandit problems to multi-state RL problems, specifically before explaining why Q-learning is needed.
suggested_caption: CliffWorld grid environment: agent navigates from start to goal avoiding cliff. (Neuromatch Academy)

description: Four-panel visualization of a trained Q-learning agent in CliffWorld: (1) top-left shows the Q-table as a heatmap across states and actions, highlighting that moving right from start or down above the cliff has very negative values; (2) other panels likely show learning curves, episode rewards, and/or policy visualization over training.
teaching_value: Allows students to inspect what the Q-table actually learns — connecting the abstract update rule to concrete numerical outcomes. The heatmap makes it clear which state-action pairs are dangerous, reinforcing understanding of how Q-values encode long-term consequences.
when_to_show: Show after students implement the Q-learning update rule, to help them interpret and validate their results and understand what a converged Q-table looks like.
suggested_caption: Q-learning results: Q-table heatmap and learning metrics after training in CliffWorld. (Neuromatch Academy)

description: Visualization output comparing SARSA algorithm performance in CliffWorld, likely showing Q-table values, episode rewards, and/or the learned policy path. Illustrates how SARSA's on-policy updates (using epsilon-greedy next action) differ from Q-learning's off-policy updates (using max Q-value).
teaching_value: Enables direct comparison between SARSA and Q-learning outcomes, helping students understand how the choice of TD target (on-policy vs off-policy) affects the learned policy and value estimates, especially near dangerous cliff states.
when_to_show: Show after students implement SARSA and run it on CliffWorld, prompting comparison with Q-learning results to highlight behavioral differences near the cliff.
suggested_caption: SARSA learning results in CliffWorld: on-policy TD updates yield a safer learned path. (Neuromatch Academy)
